title: 2025/10/23 - JU [Part 2]
displayTitle: 2025-10-23 - Justice Committee - Summary Offences (Demonstrations Near Residential Premises) Amendment Bill [Part 2]
topic: Summary Offences (Demonstrations Near Residential Premises) Amendment Bill
description: Thursday 23 October 2025 | Justice Committee Summary Offences (Demonstrations Near Residential Premises) Amendment Bill Submitters Dr Alvina Edwards Teirangi Klever Palestine Solidarity Network Aotearoa - John Minto, Co-Chair People Against Prisons Aotearoa - Toma Pearce, Parliamentary Advocacy Co-ordinator
descriptionSummary: Summary Offences (Demonstrations Near Residential Premises) Amendment Bill. Dr Alvina Edwards. Teirangi Klever

Um, I am a poet. My pu comes from my ku so I'm gonna tell a story. It's winter in Wellington, almost 2000 or so. People gather outside parliament and begin marching up Molesworth Street to stand against a racist regime on the other side of the world that's denying millions of people their freedoms.

The police wait for them at the top and form a wall of blue and battens. They command the crowd to stop, but the people in front can't. There's nowhere to go. The tide behind them keeps moving and the officers call it defiance. And then the air cracks, the battens swing people reel, and the sound of pain and protests collide in the dark, blood streaks down their faces and pull on the pavement.

And still through wind and sirens, shame carries on chanting voices through the cold air. And while that's happening here on our streets in our city, our prime minister is in London celebrating a royal wedding. Charles and Diana's fairytale plays across TV screens while Wellington streets run red with the blood of people demanding justice.

This was in 1981, the injustice was apartheid in South Africa. And back then those people are called agitators, radicals, troublemakers. But decades later, we honored them because they stood up when this house would not, they showed us something truly timeless. That moral courage does not come from those in power.

It comes from the people. And that those in power has always have always chosen silence until they no longer could. And that is exactly what this bill conveniently forgets. It tells us that protest is fine as long as it's tidy, distant, and does not disturb anyone important. It says we agree with your right to speak, but not if it might actually be heard.

And while it talks about privacy, we already have many laws that protect people from harassment, from trespassing and intimidation. This bill criminalizes perception and makes it an offense to cause unreasonable disruption. A phrase by the way that lives entirely in the eye of whoever feels uncomfortable in that given moment. You're hiding behind them and punishing visibility. You're saying the problem is not inequality, it is not injustice, is that someone had the nerve to notice those things. And this house time and time again has shown that it does not want to know why people are protesting. Right. Because knowing that would mean, God forbid, you must do something about it.

It's between power and accountability between those who govern and those who give consent to be governed. And when power rigs the scales in its own favor, what you get is not calm. What you get is submission. And look around at the world today, governments everywhere are tightening the rules on protest.

And the justifications all sound the same. The bill fits the same global pattern, a slow, deliberate shrinking of democratic value. So I have to ask this committee plainly, do you really want to be remembered for this meeting calls of justice? Not with empathy, but with reactionary and cowardly legislation?

Because once we start down that path, it's pretty hard to turn back. Protest is messy, it's disruptive, it's inconvenient, it's necessary. Every movement that is bent, the moral arc of this country toward justice has done so by speaking truth to power. It's what gives this country a spine. It's what keeps those in parliament honest when they forget who they serve.

And that's exactly why this bill is so insidious. It's designed to make protests invisible, to push dissent out of sight to the edges of public life where those in power do not have to look at it. The danger to democracy is not people demanding justice. It is politicians who are too afraid to listen. It's my recommendation that the committee should reject this undemocratic waste of time and resources.
